title: Should a Machine Ring the Bells?
summary: A town councillor wants a recording to replace the old bell ringer, but the bell ringer sees a hidden cost.

body.speaker: Council
body.text: Tomas, thank you for coming. I will be honest with you. The town wants to save money. A speaker on the tower can play the bell sound. It is cheaper than paying a person every week.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: I understand the money problem. I am not a young man, and I climb two hundred steps every Sunday. But a recording is not the same. The real bells change with the weather. On a wet day, the sound is soft and heavy.

body.speaker: Council
body.text: Most people will not hear the difference. They are busy. They want the time, not a music lesson. A machine never gets sick and never asks for a holiday.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: That is true. The machine is reliable. But I do more than ring the bells. When someone dies, I ring slowly. When a baby is born, I ring fast and bright. The town knows the news before the newspaper does.

body.speaker: Council
body.text: I had not thought of that. So the bells are a kind of message. Still, we could program the machine to play different sounds for different days.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: You could. But who will press the buttons? Someone must still know the town. The machine does not know that Mrs. Berg was loved, or that the baker's son came home from the war. A recording will play, but it will not feel.

body.speaker: Council
body.text: You are making this harder for me, Tomas. I came to save money, and now I am thinking about feelings. Maybe we can keep you for the special days and use the machine for the simple hours.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: That is a fair idea. I am not against the machine. I am old, and one day I will stop. I only ask this: do not let the town forget that the bells once spoke for living people. Teach someone, even if the machine helps.

annotations.meaningVi: một chiếc loa trên tháp chuông
rẻ hơn việc trả công cho một người

annotations.phrase: a recording is not the same
annotations.meaningVi: bản thu âm thì không giống vậy
cái máy thì đáng tin cậy
nó sẽ không có cảm xúc

annotations.phrase: the bells once spoke for living people
annotations.meaningVi: tiếng chuông từng nói thay cho những người còn sống

grammarNotes.title: Comparatives with 'than'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng tính từ cộng -er hoặc 'cheaper' với 'than' để so sánh hai thứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "It is cheaper than paying a person every week" compares the machine to a person.

grammarNotes.title: Future with 'will' for promises and predictions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'will' để dự đoán hoặc hứa hẹn về tương lai.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "one day I will stop" and "A recording will play" both use 'will' for the future.

grammarNotes.title: Modal 'could' for suggestions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'could' để đưa ra một gợi ý hoặc khả năng nhẹ nhàng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "we could program the machine to play different sounds" makes a polite suggestion.

translationVi:
Council: Tomas, cảm ơn ông đã đến. Tôi sẽ thành thật với ông. Thị trấn muốn tiết kiệm tiền. Một chiếc loa trên tháp chuông có thể phát tiếng chuông. Nó rẻ hơn việc trả công cho một người mỗi tuần.
Tomas: Tôi hiểu vấn đề tiền bạc. Tôi không còn trẻ, và tôi leo hai trăm bậc thang mỗi Chủ nhật. Nhưng bản thu âm thì không giống vậy. Chuông thật thay đổi theo thời tiết. Vào ngày mưa, âm thanh êm và nặng.
Council: Hầu hết mọi người sẽ không nghe ra sự khác biệt. Họ bận. Họ muốn biết giờ, chứ không phải một bài học âm nhạc. Một cái máy thì không bao giờ ốm và không bao giờ xin nghỉ phép.
Tomas: Điều đó đúng. Cái máy thì đáng tin cậy. Nhưng tôi làm nhiều hơn là chỉ rung chuông. Khi có người mất, tôi rung chuông chậm rãi. Khi một em bé chào đời, tôi rung nhanh và rộn ràng. Thị trấn biết tin trước cả báo chí.
Council: Tôi chưa từng nghĩ đến điều đó. Vậy tiếng chuông là một kiểu thông điệp. Dù vậy, chúng ta có thể lập trình cho cái máy phát những âm thanh khác nhau cho những ngày khác nhau.
Tomas: Ông có thể làm vậy. Nhưng ai sẽ bấm các nút? Vẫn phải có người hiểu thị trấn. Cái máy không biết rằng bà Berg được nhiều người yêu quý, hay rằng con trai người thợ làm bánh đã từ chiến trường trở về. Một bản thu âm sẽ phát, nhưng nó sẽ không có cảm xúc.
Council: Ông đang làm việc này khó hơn cho tôi đấy, Tomas. Tôi đến để tiết kiệm tiền, và giờ tôi lại đang suy nghĩ về cảm xúc. Có lẽ chúng ta có thể giữ ông cho những ngày đặc biệt và dùng cái máy cho những giờ bình thường.
Tomas: Đó là một ý tưởng hợp lý. Tôi không chống lại cái máy. Tôi đã già, và một ngày nào đó tôi sẽ dừng lại. Tôi chỉ xin điều này: đừng để thị trấn quên rằng tiếng chuông từng nói thay cho những người còn sống. Hãy dạy ai đó, ngay cả khi cái máy giúp việc.

questions.prompt: Why does the town want a machine?
What does Tomas do every Sunday?
questions.explanation: "I climb two hundred steps every Sunday."

questions.prompt: According to Tomas, what changes the sound of the real bells?
questions.explanation: "The real bells change with the weather."

questions.prompt: Why does the councillor say a machine is good?
options: It never gets sick and never asks for a holiday
It sounds better than the bells
questions.explanation: "A machine never gets sick and never asks for a holiday."

questions.prompt: How does Tomas ring the bells when someone dies?
questions.hint: Tomas talks about deaths and births.

questions.prompt: How does the town often learn the news first?
options: From the way the bells ring
questions.explanation: "The town knows the news before the newspaper does."

questions.prompt: What does the councillor suggest about the machine?
options: Program it to play different sounds for different days
questions.explanation: "we could program the machine to play different sounds for different days".
questions.hint: After the councillor calls the bells a message.

questions.prompt: What problem does Tomas see with programming the machine?
options: Someone must still know the town
questions.explanation: "who will press the buttons? Someone must still know the town."

questions.prompt: What final plan do they agree to consider?
options: Keep Tomas for special days and use the machine for simple hours
Stop ringing the bells at all
questions.explanation: "keep you for the special days and use the machine for the simple hours".

questions.prompt: When Tomas says a recording "will not feel," he means it
options: cannot understand the town's joys and sorrows
questions.explanation: The machine "does not know that Mrs. Berg was loved" — it has no feeling.
questions.hint: Tomas's reply about pressing the buttons.

questions.prompt: What is the main question in this dialogue?
options: Whether the bells are too loud
Whether the tower is safe to climb
Whether a machine can fully replace a human bell ringer
questions.explanation: They debate the recording versus Tomas's living role.

questions.prompt: How would you describe Tomas's attitude by the end?
questions.explanation: "I am not against the machine" and he calls the plan "a fair idea".

cloze.template:
Council: Tomas, thank you for coming. I will be honest with you. The town wants to {{b1}} money. A speaker on the tower can play the bell sound. It is {{b2}} than paying a person every week.
Tomas: I understand the money problem. I am not a young man, and I {{b3}} two hundred steps every Sunday. But a recording is not the same. The real bells {{b4}} with the weather. On a wet day, the sound is soft and heavy.

Tomas: That is true. The machine is {{b6}}. But I do more than ring the bells. When someone dies, I ring {{b7}}. When a baby is born, I ring fast and bright. The town knows the news before the newspaper does.
Council: I had not thought of that. So the bells are a kind of {{b8}}. Still, we could program the machine to play different sounds for different days.
Tomas: You could. But who will press the buttons? Someone must still {{b9}} the town. The machine does not know that Mrs. Berg was loved, or that the baker's son came home from the war. A recording will play, but it will not {{b10}}.
Council: You are making this harder for me, Tomas. I came to save money, and now I am thinking about feelings. Maybe we can {{b11}} you for the special days and use the machine for the simple hours.
Tomas: That is a fair idea. I am not against the machine. I am old, and one day I {{b12}} stop. I only ask this: do not let the town forget that the bells once spoke for living people. Teach someone, even if the machine helps.

blanks.explanation: Base verb after 'wants to': to save money.
Comparative with 'than': cheaper than paying a person.
Present simple plural: the bells change.
Base verb after 'will not': will not hear.
Adjective after 'is': the machine is reliable.
Base verb after 'must': must know the town.
Base verb after 'will not': will not feel.
Base verb after 'can': can keep you.
Future: one day I will stop.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Tomas says a recording "will play, but it will not feel." When a machine can do a job more cheaply, what is lost if we let it replace a person who gives the job meaning? Use the bell ringer to explain.
